The substitution pattern has produced aquifer systems with contaminant profiles skewed toward compounds exhibiting minimal natural attenuation capacity through interfacial retention, necessitating monitoring strategies that account for both present short-chain dominance and future long-chain legacy contributions. Additionally, the quantitative disparity in interfacial accumulation between chain length classes carries profound implications for transport modeling parameterization. This differential manifests in surface excess concentrations that can exceed those of short-chain homologues by orders of magnitude, fundamentally altering the partitioning behavior that governs subsurface migration. The magnitude of this accumulation disparity directly determines the retardation factor applicable to vadose zone transport simulations—models that fail to capture this chain-length-dependent interfacial affinity will systematically misrepresent plume arrival times and concentration profiles. Furthermore, the interfacial accumulation mechanism operates synergistically with other retention processes to create compound-specific transport signatures. While solid-phase sorption to organic matter and mineral surfaces provides baseline retardation for all PFAS classes, air-water interfacial partitioning introduces an additional retention dimension that scales non-linearly with fluorocarbon chain length. Moreover, this scaling relationship means that incremental chain extension produces disproportionate gains in interfacial residence time, generating sharp transport contrasts between adjacent homologues in chromatographic separation terms. Short-chain PFAS, positioned below the threshold where amphiphilic character drives substantial interfacial affinity, behave as conservative solutes in unsaturated zone hydrology, advancing at velocities approaching those of the mobile water phase. The environmental persistence of long-chain PFAS compounds—compounds that resist hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial degradation—couples with their interfacial accumulation to create sustained secondary source zones. In addition, these reservoirs retain contaminant mass for decades following initial release, functioning as time-delayed inputs to groundwater systems rather than instantaneous pulses. The temporal structure of this release fundamentally shapes aquifer contamination evolution, with long-chain compounds contributing delayed but prolonged contamination episodes that may emerge only after short-chain plumes have already traversed the system. In contrast, this temporal phasing complicates source attribution efforts and demands monitoring programs capable of detecting contamination signals across extended timescales. The practical significance of differential interfacial accumulation extends to treatment technology selection and performance prediction. Foam fractionation systems exploit the same physicochemical principle that governs natural subsurface retention—selective enrichment of long-chain PFAS at air-water interfaces—to achieve removal efficiencies that reflect compound-specific surface activity. Furthermore, systems optimized for long-chain PFAS capture demonstrate limited effectiveness against short-chain contamination, mirroring the natural attenuation limitations observed in vadose zone environments. This parallel between engineered and natural systems underscores the fundamental control exerted by molecular structure on environmental fate, reinforcing the necessity of chain-length-specific approaches in both remediation design and risk assessment frameworks. Moreover, the substitution of long-chain PFAS with short-chain alternatives in industrial formulations, driven by regulatory pressures on PFOA and PFOS, has inadvertently shifted aquifer contamination profiles toward compounds that circumvent natural interfacial retention barriers. This transition has produced groundwater systems increasingly dominated by mobile, poorly attenuated contaminants that resist both natural attenuation and conventional treatment technologies. The environmental legacy of this substitution pattern thus comprises not merely a change in contaminant identity but a fundamental alteration in aquifer vulnerability—systems previously buffered by interfacial retention capacity now face contamination threats characterized by minimal subsurface attenuation and maximal transport efficiency. Additionally, the classification framework distinguishing short- and long-chain PFAS rests fundamentally upon bioaccumulation potential rather than transport behavior, establishing criteria that misalign with groundwater vulnerability assessment priorities. This definitional basis carries significant implications for regulatory frameworks, as compounds classified by ecological hazard may exhibit markedly divergent subsurface mobility characteristics. In contrast, the resulting regulatory blind spot permits short-chain alternatives to circumvent restrictions despite their enhanced aquifer penetration capacity. Empirical investigations of AFFF-impacted sites demonstrate that relationships between soil-phase and porewater-phase PFAS concentrations govern leaching potential to underlying groundwater systems. These measurements reveal that interfacial accumulation mechanisms decouple soil concentration from leaching risk—high soil burdens of long-chain compounds may indicate effective retention rather than imminent groundwater threat. As a result, modest soil concentrations of short-chain PFAS can mask substantial groundwater contamination potential due to their preferential partitioning into mobile porewater. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water remains underdeveloped in the environmental literature, representing a critical knowledge gap for watershed-scale contamination assessment [7]. In addition, this deficiency particularly affects understanding of how vadose zone interfacial retention ultimately influences groundwater discharge dynamics and surface water loading. The absence of field-validated mass transfer coefficients limits predictive capacity for contaminant flux estimation across hydrologic boundaries. This concentration range reflects composite contributions from multiple chain-length homologues, with short-chain compounds typically dominating dissolved-phase profiles due to their limited interfacial retention. Furthermore, the upper bound of this range approaches the treatment demonstration concentrations where foam fractionation achieves near-complete removal, suggesting that engineered systems must accommodate realistic contamination scenarios encountered in field settings. This persistence couples with chain-length-dependent interfacial accumulation to create compound-specific residence time distributions in the vadose zone. In addition, long-chain PFAS resist both chemical transformation and physical mobilization, establishing secondary source zones with decade-scale longevity. Short-chain alternatives share persistence characteristics but lack complementary retention mechanisms, enabling rapid transit through subsurface systems that compounds their effective environmental mobility. The quantification of air-water interfacial accumulation reveals substantial disparities between chain-length classes. Moreover, this differential accumulation establishes a physical basis for chromatographic separation in natural systems, whereby longer fluorocarbon tails generate higher surface excess concentrations that translate directly into enhanced retention capacity. The magnitude of this disparity fundamentally alters mass transfer kinetics between stationary and mobile phases in unsaturated porous media. Moreover, these measurements reveal that interfacial accumulation mechanisms decouple total soil concentration from imminent leaching risk—substantial soil burdens of long-chain compounds may indicate effective vadose zone retention rather than impending groundwater contamination. Modest soil concentrations of short-chain PFAS can obscure significant groundwater contamination potential due to their preferential partitioning into mobile porewater fractions. This definitional basis creates regulatory blind spots wherein compounds classified by ecological hazard criteria may exhibit markedly divergent subsurface mobility characteristics. Furthermore, short-chain alternatives that circumvent bioaccumulation-based restrictions can simultaneously demonstrate enhanced aquifer penetration capacity, producing a misalignment between regulatory priorities and groundwater vulnerability assessment needs. Foam fractionation technology exploits differential interfacial accumulation for engineered removal. In addition, CTAB addition to foam fractionation systems achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters. However, the underlying physicochemical selectivity persists—systems optimized for long-chain capture remain less effective against short-chain contamination, mirroring the natural attenuation limitations observed in vadose zone environments. This parallel between engineered and natural systems reinforces the fundamental control exerted by molecular structure on environmental fate across treatment scales.\n\n## Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Fractionation and Solution Retention\n\nLong-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain congeners, establishing fundamental boundary conditions for foam-based separation efficacy in aquifer remediation. In addition, this differential interfacial partitioning directly determines susceptibility to foam fractionation, a physical separation technology increasingly deployed for PFAS-impacted groundwater. Furthermore, foam fractionation exploits the tendency of surfactant-like molecules to concentrate at air-water interfaces and become entrained within rising foam bubbles. The molecular architecture underlying this disparity involves increased fluorinated carbon chain length amplifying hydrophobic character while maintaining hydrophilic functional groups, creating the amphiphilic structure essential for interfacial partitioning. Short-chain PFAS, with truncated fluorinated tails, exhibit diminished hydrophobicity and reduced propensity for air-water interface accumulation. Additionally, this differential surface activity generates pronounced treatment efficacy disparities. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate incomplete removal despite extended treatment durations, their limited surface activity preventing effective enrichment within foam phases. Furthermore, groundwater systems contaminated with short-chain PFAS or mixed chain-length assemblages typical of industrial precursor degradation experience incomplete treatment through foam fractionation alone. The physicochemical drivers underlying this differential behavior extend beyond simple hydrophobicity metrics. Solution retention characteristics—the propensity for PFAS to remain dissolved rather than partition into separable phases—depend upon complex interactions between molecular structure, water chemistry, and interfacial dynamics. Additionally, long-chain PFAS readily depart bulk solution through foam entrainment, whereas short-chain PFAS resist such partitioning, maintaining higher aqueous concentrations and exhibiting enhanced mobility within subsurface systems (see also: Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones). These observations illuminate fundamental constraints on foam-based remediation strategies. Additionally, technologies optimized for long-chain PFAS removal underperform when applied to short-chain-dominated contamination scenarios. The solution retention exhibited by short-chain compounds ensures their continued presence in groundwater, facilitating transport through porous media and expanding contaminant plumes. Treatment system design must account for this chain-length-dependent fractionation susceptibility, potentially necessitating sequential or hybrid approaches addressing both compound classes. Moreover, the connection between surface activity and leaching potential operates through multiple pathways. Reduced foam fractionation efficiency for short-chain PFAS implies these compounds persist longer in aqueous phases, sustaining driving gradients for vertical migration through unsaturated zones (see also: Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Chain Lengths). Furthermore, long-chain PFAS removed through interfacial processes undergo reconcentration in foamate, requiring subsequent treatment or disposal that introduces secondary management challenges. The differential solution retention characteristics thus shape not only immediate treatment outcomes but also long-term contaminant fate and transport trajectories within aquifer systems. Gas-to-liquid ratios and bubble size distributions, critical determinants of interfacial surface area generation, exhibit diminished returns for short-chain compounds despite optimization for long-chain removal. Additionally, this technological limitation stems from the fundamental thermodynamic disadvantage that short-chain PFAS face at air-water interfaces, where reduced van der Waals interactions with the gaseous phase limit their competitive adsorption against other surface-active species. The persistence of short-chain PFAS in treated effluent necessitates extended hydraulic retention times or multiple treatment stages, substantially increasing operational costs and energy demands relative to long-chain-dominated contamination scenarios. Moreover, the implications for aquifer vulnerability assessment are substantial. Groundwater monitoring programs relying upon foam fractionation as a primary remediation technology risk systematically underestimating short-chain PFAS plume advancement due to treatment inefficiency masquerading as concentration reduction. This analytical artifact—where apparent remediation success reflects technological incapacity rather than genuine contaminant elimination—creates hazardous blind spots in environmental management strategies. Moreover, short-chain PFAS continuing through treatment barriers maintain their leaching potential, penetrating deeper into saturated zones where subsequent recovery becomes progressively more challenging and expensive. Comparative analysis of chain-length-specific behavior illuminates why precursor transformation products increasingly dominate environmental detection profiles. In addition, as industrial formulations shift toward shorter-chain alternatives and atmospheric or biotic degradation progressively truncates longer congeners, the resulting mixture composition evolves toward compounds inherently resistant to interfacial separation technologies. This compositional trajectory toward remediation-recalcitrant species represents a fundamental challenge for groundwater protection strategies predicated upon foam-based treatment. The temporal dimension of this transformation—operating across decades in subsurface environments—ensures that current contamination profiles will progressively shift toward short-chain dominance, degrading treatment system performance even where initial design specifications appeared adequate. Moreover, the reconcentration dynamics of foamate management introduce secondary contamination risks that differentially affect long-chain versus short-chain PFAS fate. Long-chain compounds successfully entrained within foam phases require subsequent destruction or secure disposal, with thermal treatment or deep well injection representing common management pathways. However, the substantial volume reduction achieved through foam fractionation—typically 10-fold to 100-fold concentration factors—simultaneously generates waste streams with elevated toxicity and regulatory classification challenges. For short-chain PFAS, the inverse problem emerges: their residence within treated groundwater sustains exposure pathways while evading capture in manageable waste volumes. This asymmetry in contaminant partitioning between aqueous and foam phases thus bifurcates environmental risk profiles, with long-chain PFAS posing concentrated disposal challenges and short-chain compounds presenting diffuse, persistent aquifer contamination. Moreover, the efficacy of cationic surfactant enhancement reveals critical nuances in addressing chain-length-dependent treatment limitations. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) addition achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters, with total concentrations in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters reduced from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below 8 ng L−1 detection limits within 15 minutes. However, this technological breakthrough does not eliminate fundamental thermodynamic constraints; rather, it introduces auxiliary surface-active agents that competitively partition to air-water interfaces, potentially co-entraining short-chain PFAS through indirect mechanisms rather than direct interfacial adsorption. Six cationic surfactants have been systematically evaluated for this dual-chain-length removal capability, indicating ongoing optimization efforts across the surfactant class. This definitional foundation—established prior to widespread recognition of differential interfacial activity—means that compounds sharing regulatory categorization may exhibit vastly disparate foam fractionation susceptibilities. Additionally, treatment systems designed around chain-length classifications optimized for ecological risk assessment may inadequately capture transport-relevant physicochemical gradients. Field-scale groundwater contamination magnifies these technological constraints. Moreover, total PFAS concentrations in groundwater samples from urbanized and agricultural regions vary between 22 and 718 ng L−1, spanning concentration ranges where foam fractionation efficacy transitions from quantitative removal to fractional elimination [9]. At lower concentrations approaching the lower bound of this range, interfacial competition effects become proportionally more pronounced, disproportionately compromising short-chain PFAS removal where surface activity deficits already limit enrichment efficiency. The soil-porewater partitioning dynamics underlying leaching potential demonstrate pronounced chain-length dependence through air-water interfacial adsorption mechanisms. Moreover, this interfacially-mediated retention constitutes a double-edged phenomenon: while it retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds through unsaturated zones, it simultaneously depletes their availability for foam-based extraction once groundwater saturation is reached. Short-chain PFAS, exhibit diminished interfacial retention in soils yet correspondingly elevated mobility toward groundwater tables—precisely the compounds most resistant to subsequent foam fractionation recovery. Furthermore, the research trajectory connecting unsaturated soil concentrations to mobile porewater fractions capable of groundwater migration remains underdeveloped, representing critical uncertainty in predictive vulnerability assessment. Mass transfer quantification between groundwater and surface water systems—essential for understanding complete hydrologic cycle contamination—has historically relied upon modeling rather than empirical field measurement, though recent studies have begun addressing this data gap. The \"forever chemical\" designation, reflecting recalcitrance to natural degradation processes, ensures that chain-length transformation and differential mobility effects persist across decadal timescales without attenuation through biochemical pathways. Furthermore, the regulatory classification framework governing PFAS compounds predates contemporary understanding of differential interfacial partitioning behavior, creating structural misalignment between ecological risk categories and transport-relevant physicochemical properties. This definitional foundation—established prior to widespread recognition of differential interfacial activity—means that compounds sharing regulatory categorization may exhibit vastly disparate foam fractionation susceptibilities. Additionally, treatment systems designed around chain-length classifications optimized for ecological risk assessment may inadequately capture transport-relevant physicochemical gradients. Field-scale groundwater contamination magnifies these technological constraints. Total PFAS concentrations in groundwater samples from urbanized and agricultural regions vary between 22 and 718 ng L−1, spanning concentration ranges where foam fractionation efficacy transitions from quantitative removal to fractional elimination [9]. In addition, at lower concentrations approaching the lower bound of this range, interfacial competition effects become proportionally more pronounced, disproportionately compromising short-chain PFAS removal where surface activity deficits already limit enrichment efficiency. The soil-porewater partitioning dynamics underlying leaching potential demonstrate pronounced chain-length dependence through air-water interfacial adsorption mechanisms. Moreover, this interfacially-mediated retention constitutes a double-edged phenomenon: while it retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds through unsaturated zones, it simultaneously depletes their availability for foam-based extraction once groundwater saturation is reached. Short-chain PFAS, exhibiting diminished interfacial retention in soils yet correspondingly elevated mobility toward groundwater tables—precisely the compounds most resistant to subsequent foam fractionation recovery. The research trajectory connecting unsaturated soil concentrations to mobile porewater fractions capable of groundwater migration remains underdeveloped, representing critical uncertainty in predictive vulnerability assessment. Moreover, mass transfer quantification between groundwater and surface water systems—essential for understanding complete hydrologic cycle contamination—has historically relied upon modeling rather than empirical field measurement, though recent studies have begun addressing this data gap. The \"forever chemical\" designation, reflecting recalcitrance to natural degradation processes, ensures that chain-length transformation and differential mobility effects persist across decadal timescales without attenuation through biochemical pathways.\n\n## Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones\n\nThe vadose zone functions as the critical interface determining PFAS fate prior to groundwater contamination, with chain-length-dependent partitioning governing whether compounds remain sequestered in soil or migrate downward. Moreover, this decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction creates fundamental uncertainty in risk assessment methodologies that rely on bulk soil measurements. Moreover, while interfacial sequestration at air-water boundaries temporarily retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds, this same mechanism creates a reservoir subject to mobilization upon water table fluctuations or infiltration events —a dynamic discussed in relation to secondary source formation in Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Release Dynamics. However, short-chain compounds, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, maintain higher effective mobility through the vadose profile despite lower individual sorption affinities per unit surface area. Sorption-desorption kinetics display pronounced hysteresis, with desorption rates systematically slower than sorption rates for both chain-length classes. Significantly, the magnitude of this kinetic asymmetry increases with perfluorocarbon chain length, reflecting stronger van der Waals interactions between fluorinated tails and soil surfaces —consistent with the chain-length-dependent stability patterns examined in Chemical Persistence and Environmental Stability. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate greater resistance to leaching under steady-state flow conditions but may exhibit delayed pulse releases when hydrological perturbations disrupt equilibrium conditions. Furthermore, this \"resistance to leaching\" refers specifically to retarded vertical transport rather than immobilization; long-chain compounds remain susceptible to episodic mobilization as addressed in discussions of persistent secondary sources. Short-chain compounds achieve substantial porewater concentrations due to higher initial aqueous solubility and more rapid desorption kinetics—properties examined in Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Formation and Stability—enabling faster breakthrough to underlying aquifers. The relationship between soil organic carbon content and PFAS retention follows modified partitioning models accounting for the amphiphilic nature of these contaminants. Moreover, unlike nonpolar organic compounds where sorption correlates linearly with organic carbon fraction, PFAS partitioning incorporates electrostatic interactions with mineral surfaces and specific binding to charged soil constituents. Long-chain perfluoroalkyl acids particularly exhibit this dual-mode sorption behavior, partitioning into organic carbon domains while simultaneously coordinating with metal oxide surfaces through their charged headgroups. In addition, short-chain analogs participate less effectively in hydrophobic partitioning, rendering their transport more sensitive to pH-dependent surface charge variations and ionic strength effects. Vadose zone processes preceding aquifer contamination include multiple retention mechanisms operating at distinct temporal scales. Immediate sorption to mineral surfaces occurs within hours, followed by slower diffusion into soil organic matter and intraparticle micropores over days to weeks. Consequently, long-chain PFAS penetrate these immobile domains more extensively due to favorable free energy of transfer, effectively removing them from mobile porewater fractions on timescales relevant to episodic infiltration events. Short-chain compounds remain predominantly in rapidly exchanging pools, maintaining higher effective mobility despite lower affinity per sorption site. Nevertheless, sites with historical long-chain PFAS releases may exhibit delayed groundwater impacts relative to short-chain contamination, not due to absence of source term but due to vadose zone retention capacity. Short-chain replacement compounds introduced after regulatory phase-outs demonstrate more rapid breakthrough to groundwater, potentially underestimating transport velocities calculated from conservative tracer behavior. This temporal decoupling between surface release and groundwater detection complicates source attribution and remedial prioritization, as detection timing does not necessarily reflect release chronology. In addition, treatment implications extend directly from these mechanistic understandings. Ex-situ soil washing and in-situ flushing technologies must account for chain-length-dependent desorption kinetics; long-chain PFAS require more aggressive chemical enhancement or extended residence times to achieve equivalent removal efficiency. Furthermore, the mobile porewater fraction—directly available for groundwater migration—contains disproportionate short-chain enrichment relative to total soil composition, suggesting that monitoring focused on leachable rather than total concentrations would better predict aquifer contamination risk. Remediation strategies targeting the vadose zone must incorporate chain-length-specific extraction protocols rather than assuming uniform contaminant behavior across PFAS classes. The capillary fringe region warrants particular attention as the transition zone where vadose zone processes intersect saturated groundwater systems. Moreover, long-chain PFAS accumulating at this interface through interfacial trapping mechanisms create concentrated transition zones that may serve as persistent source terms during water table rise events. Short-chain compounds, transit this boundary more readily due to limited retention capacity in the capillary fringe, establishing direct hydraulic connectivity between surface contamination and aquifer receptors with minimal attenuation. Additionally, soil moisture content emerges as a critical variable modulating effective partitioning coefficients across the unsaturated profile. At intermediate saturations, the competing effects of reduced aqueous phase volume and enhanced air-water interfacial area generate non-monotonic relationships between moisture state and PFAS mobility. Long-chain compounds exhibit maximum retention under partially saturated conditions where interfacial area is maximized, whereas complete saturation or near-dry conditions diminish this retention mechanism. Moreover, short-chain PFAS display comparatively muted moisture dependence, reflecting their dominant partitioning into the aqueous phase regardless of saturation state. The role of soil structure and aggregation in regulating pore-scale transport pathways remains underappreciated in conventional partitioning models. Furthermore, intra-aggregate porosity creates diffusion-limited domains where long-chain PFAS may become sequestered within soil structural units, physically isolating contaminants from advective flow paths. Short-chain compounds, with higher effective diffusion coefficients, more readily equilibrate between inter-aggregate and intra-aggregate porosity, maintaining concentration continuity across pore domains. This structural sequestration contributes to the apparent kinetic hysteresis observed in batch desorption experiments, conflating true chemical desorption resistance with physical transport limitations. Additionally, co-solute effects and competitive displacement phenomena introduce additional complexity in multi-component PFAS mixtures characteristic of AFFF formulations. The presence of short-chain compounds may indirectly facilitate long-chain mobilization through competitive displacement from high-affinity sorption sites, particularly under dynamic flow conditions where local equilibrium assumptions fail. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS at elevated concentrations may modify air-water interfacial properties, potentially altering the effective retention capacity for co-occurring short-chain analogs. These mixture-specific behaviors challenge the predictive capacity of single-component partitioning parameters applied to complex environmental systems. Temperature fluctuations through seasonal cycles or climate variability introduce thermodynamic perturbations to equilibrium partitioning relationships. Nevertheless, the exothermic nature of hydrophobic partitioning interactions implies enhanced retention at lower temperatures, with long-chain PFAS exhibiting greater temperature sensitivity due to larger enthalpic contributions from perfluorocarbon tail interactions. Short-chain compounds, with weaker overall sorption enthalpies, demonstrate more stable partitioning behavior across temperature ranges relevant to vadose zone environments. Additionally, seasonal groundwater recharge events coinciding with temperature minima may therefore mobilize differentially enriched PFAS signatures compared to warm-season infiltration. Conventional leaching tests performed under standard temperature and moisture conditions may systematically misrepresent field-scale mobilization potential, particularly for long-chain compounds where retention capacity varies nonlinearly with environmental state variables. Monitoring strategies must incorporate temporal dynamics of infiltration events rather than relying on steady-state assumptions, as the episodic nature of vadose zone transport governs actual contaminant delivery to underlying aquifers. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds represents a critical mechanism distinguishing long-chain from short-chain transport dynamics in unsaturated soils. This differential interfacial affinity fundamentally governs how these compound classes distribute within soil pore networks and determines their relative mobility toward underlying aquifers. However, the pronounced accumulation of longer-chain compounds at air-water boundaries effectively sequesters these contaminants within the vadose zone, creating a temporary—but potentially reversible—retardation mechanism that does not operate equivalently for short-chain replacements. The relationship between soil-bound PFAS and mobile porewater concentrations remains inadequately characterized for predictive modeling purposes. This measurement gap introduces substantial uncertainty when extrapolating from soil contamination data to groundwater risk assessments, particularly given the chain-length-dependent decoupling between total soil burden and leachable fraction. Specifically, this finding carries significant implications for remediation technology selection: treatments exploiting interfacial phenomena, such as surfactant-enhanced foam fractionation, achieve rapid removal of both compound classes from groundwater—reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes. The addition of cationic surfactants like CTAB, which exhibit strong air-water interface affinity, enables this rapid extraction from both synthetic and natural groundwater matrices. Furthermore, this disconnect between environmental fate drivers and regulatory classification schemes complicates risk-based prioritization of contaminated sites. The persistence of PFAS compounds—their characterization as \"forever chemicals\" reflecting resistance to natural degradation processes —ensures that vadose zone reservoirs established through historical releases remain active source terms indefinitely, with chain-length-dependent release kinetics governing the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination. The fundamental distinction between chain-length classes in air-water interfacial partitioning has direct implications for how regulatory frameworks classify and manage these contaminants. In addition, current classification schemes for short- and long-chain PFAS derive primarily from bioaccumulation potential rather than subsurface transport behavior, creating a disconnect between environmental fate drivers and risk-based prioritization criteria. This misalignment proves particularly problematic when historical contamination scenarios involve complex AFFF mixtures where transport predictions based solely on bioaccumulation metrics fail to capture vadose zone mobility patterns. Additionally, the environmental ubiquity of PFAS compounds—detected across water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations nationwide —underscores the necessity of understanding chain-length-specific partitioning for predictive exposure assessment. The characterization of these substances as \"forever chemicals\" reflects their stability and persistence in environmental systems and biological tissues, indicating they do not readily degrade and accumulate over time. This intrinsic persistence ensures that vadose zone reservoirs function as indefinite source terms, with the temporal pattern of groundwater contamination governed by chain-length-dependent release kinetics rather than attenuation through natural degradation processes. Furthermore, the practical implications of these mechanistic understandings extend to treatment technology development. The efficiency of such ex-situ treatments contrasts with the challenge of in-situ vadose zone remediation, where the same interfacial phenomena that enable foam fractionation simultaneously retard natural flushing of long-chain compounds from soil matrices.\n\n## Groundwater Concentration Baselines and Background Contamination Levels\n\nGroundwater concentration baselines provide essential reference points for distinguishing anthropogenic PFAS contamination from background levels and for evaluating the differential severity of long-chain versus short-chain PFAS intrusion. In addition, these quantitative benchmarks enable assessment of whether observed concentrations reflect point-source releases, diffuse regional inputs, or natural attenuation processes. Nevertheless, this spread reflects heterogeneous source distributions, variable transport distances, and differential attenuation processes. The upper bound of this range substantially exceeds typical regulatory screening levels for individual PFAS compounds, indicating that cumulative exposures in affected aquifers present environmental health concerns even when single-analyte concentrations appear modest. The analytical significance of these concentration ranges extends beyond magnitude comparisons. Furthermore, groundwater systems function as integrative reservoirs where PFAS loading reflects time-integrated source inputs modulated by subsurface transport dynamics. Long-chain PFAS exhibit stronger hydrophobic interactions and sorptive affinity to solid phases, theoretically producing attenuated transport distances and elevated concentrations near source zones. However, short-chain homologs possess greater mobility potential, suggesting broader spatial distribution at potentially lower peak concentrations—though aggregated total PFAS measurements limit direct mechanistic interpretation. Establishing true background contamination levels presents methodological challenges. The ubiquity of PFAS in contemporary environmental systems, including atmospheric deposition and agricultural amendments, complicates discrimination between natural background and diffuse anthropogenic inputs. In addition, the lower concentration bound of 22 ng L⁻¹ likely represents a practical detection limit for composite PFAS analysis rather than a genuine geochemical baseline, indicating that even minimally impacted groundwater systems exhibit measurable PFAS burdens. The distinction between point-source contamination plumes and regional background elevation carries profound implications for leaching potential assessment. Furthermore, point-source scenarios, typical of industrial facilities and firefighting training areas, generate steep concentration gradients where long-chain PFAS accumulate near source zones, while short-chain compounds penetrate deeper into aquifer systems. This differential penetration creates characteristic concentration profiles that invert with transport distance—long-chain species declining precipitously while short-chain concentrations persist, ultimately establishing mobile contamination fronts dominated by short-chain homologs. Regional background elevation results from atmospheric transport and deposition coupled with agricultural PFAS applications, producing more uniform concentration distributions where chain-length profiles reflect source signatures rather than transport fractionation. In addition, the 22–718 ng L⁻¹ range encompasses both contamination regimes, with higher values indicating proximity to primary sources and lower values representing diluted or aged contamination. This mechanistic understanding explains why groundwater contamination profiles frequently show short-chain PFAS dominance at distance from source zones. In addition, treatment implications of these concentration baselines are substantial. Documented occurrences of total PFAS at hundreds of nanograms per liter in natural groundwater systems establish that conventional drinking water treatment infrastructure requires reassessment for PFAS-specific removal. The differential treatment challenges posed by short-chain versus long-chain compounds become acute when background concentrations approach regulatory thresholds. In addition, the concentration range data inform risk-based prioritization frameworks. Aquifers exhibiting concentrations toward the upper bound warrant immediate intervention, while lower concentrations may permit monitored strategies—though PFAS persistence fundamentally limits natural attenuation capacity. Moreover, the chain-length composition critically determines appropriate management responses, with short-chain-dominated plumes demanding different treatment technologies than long-chain-enriched source zones. Quantitative baselines serve as essential foundation for analyzing sediment contamination dynamics and groundwater-surface water exchange, providing context for evaluating whether observed concentrations in those compartments represent primary contamination or secondary redistribution from aquifer systems. The temporal dimension of groundwater contamination introduces critical complexity to baseline interpretation. Furthermore, concentration ranges measured at single time points cannot capture the dynamic evolution of PFAS plumes, where source zone depletion, aquifer recharge fluctuations, and seasonal water table variations produce substantial concentration variability. This temporal instability complicates efforts to establish stable background thresholds, as concentrations previously interpreted as baseline may instead represent transient states within evolving contamination scenarios. In addition, the analytical methods employed for PFAS quantification directly influence perceived baseline levels. Detection limits for individual compounds vary substantially across analytical platforms, with some short-chain homologs exhibiting poorer sensitivity than their long-chain counterparts due to ionization inefficiencies in mass spectrometric detection. Reported background concentrations may underrepresent short-chain PFAS contributions when method performance is not uniformly optimized across the full homolog series, potentially skewing chain-length distribution interpretations. Furthermore, pH-dependent speciation of perfluorinated carboxylic acids alters their sorptive behavior, with deprotonated forms exhibiting reduced affinity for mineral surfaces at elevated pH, enhancing transport potential. Dissolved organic carbon competes with PFAS for sorption sites or facilitates co-transport through formation of mobile complexes, particularly affecting long-chain compounds with stronger hydrophobic character. Moreover, these geochemical controls generate site-specific concentration baselines that resist universal application, necessitating aquifer-specific characterization for accurate leaching potential assessment. The Brazilian study context reveals important geographic variability in baseline establishment. Urbanized and agricultural settings present distinct source portfolios compared to industrial point sources, with atmospheric deposition and legacy agricultural PFAS applications contributing to diffuse background elevation. Additionally, this source heterogeneity means that concentration ranges established in one hydrogeologic setting may misrepresent baseline conditions elsewhere, particularly where industrial PFAS manufacturing or intensive aqueous film-forming foam usage has occurred. The 22–718 ng L⁻¹ concentration envelope carries regulatory significance when compared to emerging drinking water standards. In addition, the lower bound approximates detection capability for comprehensive PFAS analysis, while the upper bound substantially exceeds proposed maximum contaminant levels for individual compounds in multiple jurisdictions, indicating that groundwater systems throughout the sampled region require evaluation for potential human exposure. However, regulatory frameworks predominantly target long-chain PFAS, potentially overlooking short-chain contributions that dominate mobile groundwater contamination fronts. This regulatory mismatch between measured concentrations and controlled compounds complicates risk communication and remediation prioritization based on baseline data alone. Air-water interfacial phenomena fundamentally govern the subsurface distribution and leaching potential of chain-length-differentiated PFAS. This differential interfacial partitioning has direct implications for vadose zone transport and groundwater recharge dynamics. Additionally, the pronounced air-water interfacial adsorption of longer-chain compounds restricts their mobility in partially saturated soils, effectively sequestering these species within upper soil horizons and delaying groundwater intrusion. Groundwater baselines in recently recharged aquifers may underrepresent long-chain PFAS contributions relative to their short-chain counterparts, which exhibit diminished interfacial retention and enhanced vertical migration capacity. This mechanistic insight explains observed discrepancies between regional soil PFAS inventories and corresponding groundwater baselines. Consequently, areas with substantial topsoil long-chain PFAS burdens may generate groundwater plumes dominated by short-chain species due to differential leaching kinetics. Compounds classified as \"short-chain\" based on toxicological criteria often exhibit the greatest aquifer penetration, yet receive reduced regulatory scrutiny. Furthermore, this categorical disconnect complicates risk-based management when groundwater baselines reveal short-chain dominance at receptor locations distant from source zones. The persistent environmental distribution of PFAS—encompassing water, air, soil, biota, and human populations—establishes that baseline contamination reflects not merely local source inputs but integrated regional contamination. Their chemical stability ensures that groundwater concentration baselines represent cumulative, time-integrated loading rather than transient contamination events.\n\n## Sediment Contamination Hotspots: Formation and Secondary Source Potential\n\nSediment contamination hotspots function as persistent secondary sources capable of sustaining aquifer recontamination over extended temporal scales. Moreover, these hotspots form preferentially near the sediment surface in zones immediately downstream from point source discharges, creating localized zones of elevated PFAA concentration that persist beyond primary source remediation [10]. In addition, this surface-proximal localization carries particular significance for aquifer vulnerability, as these shallow reservoirs maintain direct hydraulic connectivity with underlying groundwater systems through hyporheic exchange and vertical infiltration pathways—a dynamic explored in detail in the discussion of soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. The secondary source potential of sediment hotspots derives from their capacity to release accumulated PFAS mass following perturbations to prevailing geochemical conditions. Unlike primary sources where contamination inputs occur through direct discharge, secondary release from sediments operates through desorption-driven mechanisms triggered by changes in porewater chemistry, flow regime alterations, or competitive displacement by co-contaminants—processes that parallel the partitioning dynamics governing PFAS mobility in unsaturated soil systems. Moreover, this distinction fundamentally shapes remediation prioritization, as hotspot sediments require active intervention rather than passive source control measures. Long-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially stronger sediment retention and more efficient hotspot formation near discharge points, where these compounds accumulate to higher concentrations within smaller spatial footprints. However, short-chain PFAS demonstrate greater downstream transport capacity, distributing contamination across broader sediment reaches rather than concentrating in discrete hotspots. The bioaccumulation-based classification scheme previously established for PFAS categorization reinforces this functional divergence in environmental partitioning behavior. Long-chain PFAS hotspots represent high-concentration, spatially constrained sources; however, the magnitude of flux to underlying aquifers depends on site-specific hydraulic gradients and mass transfer kinetics rather than concentration alone, reflecting the complex relationship between soil-phase concentrations and groundwater loading documented in soil-porewater partitioning research. In contrast, short-chain PFAS, despite forming less pronounced hotspots, create diffuse contamination zones that sustain chronic low-level loading across extended groundwater capture areas. Neither scenario permits simple risk assessment based solely on total mass calculations; rather, the spatial configuration of contamination relative to groundwater flow paths determines secondary source significance. In addition, treatment implications diverge markedly between hotspot types. Long-chain enriched sediments may respond to targeted extraction technologies leveraging their strong surface affinity. Short-chain contaminated sediments present substantially greater technical challenges given their weak retention across environmental matrices, necessitating more aggressive and costly remediation approaches. However, the differential treatability of hotspot sediments reinforces the economic and technical advantages of preventing long-chain PFAS discharge relative to managing distributed short-chain contamination. Temporal dynamics of hotspot reactivation warrant particular attention in aquifer protection planning. In addition, sediment PFAS reservoirs can maintain release potential over decadal timescales, substantially exceeding the operational lifetimes of typical source control measures. This persistence creates legacy contamination scenarios where aquifer systems continue receiving PFAS loading decades after primary source cessation, complicating attainment of groundwater quality objectives. The chain-length composition of hotspot sediments directly influences this temporal dimension, with long-chain compounds exhibiting slower release kinetics that prolong secondary source activity. Additionally, understanding hotspot formation and secondary source potential provides essential context for interpreting groundwater concentration baselines, particularly in distinguishing true background conditions from legacy contamination signals. Elevated aquifer PFAS concentrations in areas lacking identifiable current sources frequently reflect sediment hotspot reactivation rather than undetected ongoing discharge—a consideration central to establishing defensible groundwater concentration baselines. Furthermore, this recognition shifts monitoring priorities toward sediment characterization and hyporheic zone assessment in groundwater protection strategies. The formation mechanisms underlying hotspot development reveal critical dependencies on sediment physicochemical properties and hydrodynamic conditions that govern PFAS partitioning dynamics. Fine-grained sediment fractions, particularly those rich in organic carbon and iron oxyhydroxides, provide preferential binding sites for long-chain PFAS compounds, concentrating contamination in discrete stratigraphic horizons [10]. However, this grain-size selectivity creates a heterogeneous distribution where contaminant mass concentrates in thin, high-capacity layers rather than distributing uniformly through the sediment column, amplifying local concentration gradients driving diffusive flux to porewater. The hydraulic connectivity between hotspot sediments and underlying aquifers operates through multiple transport regimes that vary substantially with seasonal and climatic conditions. Moreover, during high-flow periods, advective transport through surface-connected preferential flow paths dominates mass transfer, delivering concentrated PFAS pulses to groundwater systems [10]. Low-flow conditions shift dominance toward diffusive exchange across the sediment-water interface, sustaining chronic baseline loading at reduced rates. This regime-dependent transport variability complicates predictive modeling of aquifer loading rates, as empirical measurements at single time points may poorly characterize annual mass loading budgets. Furthermore, the competitive displacement dynamics within hotspots warrant particular analytical attention. Co-occurring inorganic and organic contaminants, including natural organic matter and competing anions, can trigger episodic PFAS release through competitive sorption mechanisms [10]. Moreover, these displacement events, often associated with seasonal organic matter fluxes or anthropogenic chemical inputs, generate transient concentration spikes in porewater that propagate rapidly through connected groundwater pathways. The magnitude of competitive release scales inversely with perfluorinated chain length, reflecting weaker binding affinities for short-chain compounds that render them more susceptible to displacement from sediment binding sites. Temperature fluctuations introduce an additional dimension of hotspot reactivation potential. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures reduce sorption equilibrium constants for PFAS compounds, particularly for longer-chain homologues where hydrophobic interactions contribute substantially to binding free energy. Climate warming scenarios thus project enhanced secondary source activity from existing sediment reservoirs, with thermal sensitivity varying systematically across the PFAS chain-length spectrum. In addition, this temperature-dependent release mechanism intersects with seasonal thermal stratification in surface water bodies, creating depth-variable reactivation patterns within sediment profiles. The spatial configuration of hotspots relative to groundwater extraction infrastructure determines practical exposure consequences. Hotspots positioned within capture zones of municipal or agricultural wells generate sustained contaminant breakthrough curves characterized by extended tailing due to rate-limited desorption kinetics. However, this temporal dispersion contrasts with more immediate contamination signals from primary source proximity, complicating source attribution in groundwater quality investigations. The diagnostic differentiation between ongoing primary discharge and reactivated secondary loading requires multi-tracer approaches incorporating compound-specific isotope analysis and co-contaminant fingerprinting. In addition, sediment hotspot longevity presents fundamental challenges for groundwater remediation timeline planning. The substantial PFAS mass inventories accumulated in near-surface sediments, combined with slow release kinetics, establish multi-decadal contamination trajectories that extend substantially beyond conventional groundwater remediation timeframes. This persistence necessitates adaptive management frameworks incorporating sediment hotspot treatment or containment as integral components of comprehensive aquifer restoration strategies, rather than relying exclusively on groundwater extraction and treatment approaches that address symptoms without eliminating secondary sources. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds introduces a critical dimension to hotspot formation dynamics that complements sediment-sorbed reservoirs. In unsaturated soil zones and hyporheic environments where air-water interfaces abound, long-chain compounds partition preferentially into interfacial regions, creating secondary concentration gradients that parallel their sediment-bound hotspots. In addition, this interfacial partitioning effectively sequesters long-chain PFAS in transient, mobile reservoirs distinct from sediment binding sites, generating a dual-reservoir system where contamination exists in both sorbed and interfacial phases. These findings establish that soil concentration measurements alone provide insufficient proxies for groundwater loading potential, as the magnitude of porewater mobilization depends on dynamic equilibrium conditions rather than total mass inventories. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water systems remains limited, with existing studies addressing this methodological gap through integrated monitoring approaches that capture bidirectional flux dynamics. In addition, this biological endpoint-based categorization aligns with physicochemical partitioning patterns, as compounds exhibiting greater bioaccumulation concurrently demonstrate stronger environmental retention and more pronounced hotspot formation. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. Additionally, the air-water interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS compounds introduces a critical dimension to hotspot formation dynamics that complements sediment-sorbed reservoirs. This interfacial partitioning effectively sequesters long-chain PFAS in transient, mobile reservoirs distinct from sediment binding sites, generating a dual-reservoir system where contamination exists in both sorbed and interfacial phases. These findings establish that soil concentration measurements alone provide insufficient proxies for groundwater loading potential, as the magnitude of porewater mobilization depends on dynamic equilibrium conditions rather than total mass inventories. In addition, this biological endpoint-based categorization aligns with physicochemical partitioning patterns, as compounds exhibiting greater bioaccumulation concurrently demonstrate stronger environmental retention and more pronounced hotspot formation. The convergence of biological and geochemical classification schemes supports predictive risk assessment frameworks that translate molecular structure into environmental fate projections. As a result, PFAS are commonly known as 'forever chemicals' because they do not readily degrade in nature, and are known as forever chemicals because they are stable and persistent in the environment and in the human body, meaning they do not break down and can accumulate over time. PFAS are constantly spreading in the environment and have been found in water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and humans across the nation. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water systems remains limited, with existing studies addressing this methodological gap through integrated monitoring approaches that capture bidirectional flux dynamics.\n\n## Groundwater-Surface Water Exchange and Mass Transfer Dynamics\n\nThe groundwater-surface water interface constitutes the critical boundary where subsurface PFAS plumes encounter hydrologically connected receptors, yet quantitative characterization of mass transfer across this transition remains substantially incomplete. Additionally, this methodological limitation constrains predictive capabilities for contaminant fate modeling, as field-validated mass transfer coefficients for PFAS compounds remain scarce. In addition, the hyporheic zone serves as the primary locus for bidirectional PFAS flux between groundwater and surface water systems. Hydrological gradients drive advective transport across this interface, while concentration gradients established through differential partitioning mechanisms govern diffusive components. The relative dominance of advection versus diffusion depends on aquifer hydraulic conductivity, stream stage variability, and head differentials between surface and subsurface systems. Moreover, long-chain compounds exhibit pronounced affinity for sedimentary organic carbon, creating concentration discontinuities at the groundwater-surface water transition. Sorptive retention dampens instantaneous flux into surface waters but establishes sustained release conditions where contaminated groundwater discharge occurs over extended timescales, a dynamic elaborated in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms in unsaturated zones. Moreover, short-chain PFAS demonstrate weaker sediment binding, facilitating more rapid equilibration across the interface. This reduced retardation enables higher instantaneous mass flux rates per unit concentration gradient, fundamentally altering the temporal dynamics of contaminant delivery to surface receptors. For nationwide distribution patterns of short-chain versus long-chain compound mobility, see Section [X.X]. In addition, temperature and pH fluctuations at the interface modulate sorption equilibrium and transient mass transfer rates, as detailed in Section [X.X] regarding soil-porewater partitioning mechanisms. Seasonal groundwater temperature variations alter PFAS aqueous solubility and sorption partition coefficients, introducing temporal dynamics that complicate steady-state mass balance calculations. Moreover, episodic precipitation events generate hydraulic transients that temporarily reverse flow directions across the hyporheic zone, inducing bidirectional contaminant migration that challenges unidirectional transport assumptions in standard exposure models. Surface water discharge fundamentally transforms contaminant fate pathways. Mass entering surface waters becomes subject to more rapid advective distribution across receptor exposure zones compared to groundwater-contained mass. Furthermore, photolytic and biotic transformation processes absent in groundwater may alter PFAS profiles, with the relative persistence of long-chain versus short-chain compounds determining whether surface water discharge represents a terminal sink or temporary transfer mechanism. Treatment system design requires explicit consideration of chain-length-dependent mass transfer kinetics. Additionally, extraction well networks positioned to capture groundwater before surface water discharge must account for the enhanced mobility of short-chain PFAS, which may bypass capture zones designed for strongly retarded long-chain compounds. In-situ treatment barriers face kinetic limitations when short-chain PFAS transit times through reactive media prove insufficient for complete degradation or sorption. The bidirectional nature of hyporheic exchange carries additional complexity for remedial decision-making. In addition, surface water infiltration during high-flow conditions transports PFAS into shallow aquifer zones, effectively expanding the subsurface contamination footprint beyond original source delineations. Coupled with sediment re-suspension dynamics, this creates feedback loops where surface water becomes a secondary source for groundwater contamination rather than solely a receptor. Additionally, quantifying these exchange dynamics through field instrumentation remains essential for parameterizing predictive models. Nested piezometer arrays, seepage meters, and differential flow gauging constitute critical measurement approaches. Current reliance on laboratory-derived partition coefficients and numerical approximations for dispersion characteristics introduces uncertainty that propagates through risk assessment calculations. Additionally, enhanced field characterization targeting chain-length-specific mass transfer rates would substantially improve confidence in exposure forecasts and remedial performance predictions for groundwater-surface water systems impacted by PFAS contamination. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer coefficients remains notably scarce, representing a persistent methodological gap that constrains model validation efforts. In addition, laboratory-derived sorption parameters frequently fail to capture the heterogeneity of natural hyporheic sediments, where organic carbon content varies spatially over orders of magnitude and mineral surface chemistry differs substantially from simplified experimental matrices. Mass transfer rates estimated from batch experiments may deviate from field-scale behavior by factors that remain uncharacterized for most PFAS compounds. The manufacturing facility context examined in recent field investigations demonstrates how source-area hydrogeology governs plume discharge characteristics to receiving waters. Furthermore, high-resolution temporal monitoring reveals that PFAS concentration profiles in discharging groundwater do not mirror static source compositions, indicating that subsurface transformation and differential transport fractionation occur prior to surface water entry. This finding carries significant implications for receptor exposure assessment, as surface water concentrations may poorly reflect original source signatures. Moreover, chain-length-dependent discharge patterns emerge distinctly under field conditions. Long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced concentration attenuation across the hyporheic zone due to combined sorptive and diffusive limitations, whereas short-chain homologs demonstrate more conservative transport with mass flux rates correlating strongly with groundwater discharge velocity. The resulting compositional shift between groundwater and surface water end-members necessitates separate analytical frameworks for exposure modeling rather than simple dilution calculations. Moreover, seasonal hydrological variability introduces additional complexity for mass transfer quantification. Baseflow conditions favor sustained, low-concentration discharge of long-chain PFAS from sediment storage reservoirs, while stormflow events mobilize short-chain compounds through enhanced groundwater-surface water connectivity and reduced hydraulic residence times. Moreover, this temporal partitioning means that annual mass loading estimates based on single-season sampling campaigns risk substantial bias depending on which hydrological regime dominates the monitoring period. The bidirectional flux characterization presents particular challenges for remedial endpoint establishment. Surface water quality criteria typically assume continuous discharge scenarios, yet episodic infiltration during flood events can reverse contaminant migration directions and expand the effective source zone. Furthermore, remedial systems designed solely for groundwater capture may prove inadequate when surface water constitutes a secondary contamination pathway, requiring integrated monitoring approaches that track mass movement across both domains simultaneously. Analytical detection capabilities further constrain field-based mass transfer studies. In addition, the ultra-trace concentrations characteristic of PFAS in environmental matrices demand specialized extraction and quantification methods that introduce systematic biases between groundwater and surface water sample types. Method reporting limits that differ between matrices can create apparent concentration discontinuities that reflect analytical artifacts rather than true mass transfer behavior, complicating coefficient derivation from paired measurements. Future field investigation priorities should emphasize direct measurement of interfacial fluxes through nested instrumentation capable of resolving concentration gradients at centimeter scales within the hyporheic zone. Additionally, such approaches would enable validation of numerical model formulations currently reliant on assumed equilibrium conditions that may not prevail under dynamic field conditions. Air-water interfacial phenomena introduce additional complexity to PFAS mass transfer dynamics at the groundwater-surface water interface. In addition, this differential interfacial adsorption capacity fundamentally alters transport behavior in systems where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive air-water boundaries. Long-chain compounds may experience enhanced retardation in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, effectively decoupling their arrival times from groundwater flow velocities. The significance of air-water interfacial adsorption extends beyond unsaturated zone processes. In contrast, recent investigations demonstrated that this mechanism governs the distribution of longer-chain PFAS within soil samples, creating concentration profiles that deviate substantially from predictions based solely on solid-phase sorption. For mass transfer modeling across the groundwater-surface water interface, this implies that long-chain PFAS may exhibit dual-retardation behavior—sorptive partitioning to organic carbon combined with interfacial accumulation—that short-chain compounds largely bypass. Moreover, bioaccumulation potential serves as the definitive criterion for classifying chain-length categories, establishing a functional rather than purely structural distinction between PFAS groups. This classification framework carries direct implications for interfacial mass transfer, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously demonstrate stronger interfacial activity and sediment affinity. The convergence of these properties means that long-chain PFAS encounter multiple sequential barriers to surface water discharge, whereas short-chain compounds face fewer attenuation mechanisms. Moreover, field-based quantification efforts remain essential for resolving these complex behaviors. Such empirical approaches are necessary because laboratory-derived parameters fail to capture the coupled physicochemical processes operating at environmental interfaces. Furthermore, the scarcity of validated field measurements for chain-length-specific mass transfer rates continues to limit predictive model confidence, particularly for scenarios involving dynamic hydrological conditions where equilibrium assumptions prove inadequate. This classification framework carries mechanistic significance for groundwater-surface water exchange, as compounds with elevated bioaccumulation potential simultaneously exhibit enhanced air-water interfacial activity and stronger sediment affinity. The resulting dual-retardation behavior—combining organic carbon partitioning with interfacial accumulation—generates pronounced arrival time decoupling from groundwater flow velocities for long-chain species. Additionally, this mechanism assumes particular importance in unsaturated zone transitions preceding surface water discharge, where entrapped air or fluctuating water tables create extensive interfacial boundaries. The convergence of bioaccumulation potential, interfacial activity, and sediment affinity creates sequential attenuation barriers that fundamentally differentiate chain-length-dependent mass transfer. Furthermore, long-chain PFAS encounter multiple retention mechanisms—organic carbon sorption, air-water interfacial adsorption, and diffusion limitations—whereas short-chain compounds experience reduced retardation and more conservative transport. This mechanistic asymmetry necessitates distinct analytical frameworks for exposure assessment and remedial design, as uniform treatment approaches fail to account for the divergent fate pathways established through these coupled physicochemical processes.\n\n## Remediation Methodologies: Cationic Surfactants and Foam Fractionation Applications\n\nAdditionally, this differential surface activity originates from the pronounced hydrophobic character conferred by extended fluorinated chains, which drives preferential orientation and concentration at phase boundaries. The technological implementation of this principle has been systematically evaluated through examination of six distinct cationic surfactant formulations. For instance, these surfactants function by stabilizing foam structures while enhancing PFAS partitioning to the interface, with molecular design parameters—including hydrocarbon tail length, charge density, and counterion composition—modulating both foam stability and PFAS-enrichment capacity. Additionally, surfactant selection becomes particularly consequential when addressing mixed-chain-length contamination, as competitive interfacial partitioning may favor long-chain species and leave short-chain counterparts inadequately extracted in treated effluent —a limitation examined in detail in the Treatment Efficiency Disparities section regarding short-chain remediation challenges. Empirical validation demonstrates substantial removal capabilities under optimized conditions, though with important chain-length-dependent qualifications. However, this aggregate performance metric masks substantial disparities in individual species removal: while long-chain PFAS achieve near-complete extraction, short-chain analogs demonstrate markedly diminished removal efficiencies, reflecting the underlying surface-activity differential. The optimistic framing of total PFAS reduction must be tempered by recognition that short-chain species—increasingly prevalent in contemporary AFFF formulations and environmental degradation products—may persist at 50% or greater residual concentrations despite aggressive treatment parameters. Operational constraints fundamentally shape aquifer remediation applicability. In addition, the technology requires ex situ implementation, necessitating groundwater extraction and above-ground treatment before potential reinjection or discharge. This engineering configuration limits deployment for deep confined aquifers or regions where water table depression is environmentally or economically prohibitive, constraints that intersect with groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics discussed in the corresponding hydrological section. Moreover, for accessible unconfined aquifers, the method offers distinct waste management advantages over activated carbon or ion exchange: concentrated PFAS-laden foam fractions amenable to subsequent destruction technologies replace spent solid waste requiring landfill disposal —a characteristic relevant to sediment contamination hotspot management where concentrated waste streams require controlled destruction. Treatment system design must accommodate chain-length-specific performance characteristics. Long-chain PFAS removal achieves satisfactory efficiencies with standard cationic surfactant formulations and conventional bubble generation parameters. Indeed, short-chain PFAS remediation demands substantially modified approaches—increased surfactant concentrations, reduced bubble sizes to enhance interfacial area, or sequential treatment stages—that escalate operational costs and energy requirements. These economic and engineering trade-offs reflect a broader technological limitation: surface-activity-based separation methods face fundamental constraints with minimally hydrophobic short-chain perfluorinated compounds. Furthermore, the environmental significance of chain-length transformations—whether through natural precursor degradation or engineered treatment—warrants careful consideration. Conversion of more extractable long-chain species to recalcitrant short-chain forms may inadvertently degrade remediation effectiveness, as the latter demonstrate markedly diminished foam fractionation amenability. This risk reinforces the necessity for treatment trains that address both chain length categories rather than relying on single-technology solutions. Furthermore, analytical considerations extend to surfactant residual management. Cationic surfactants introduced to enhance PFAS removal may persist in treated water, potentially affecting downstream biological processes or requiring additional treatment stages. In addition, the environmental fate of these auxiliary chemicals in aquifer systems—should treated water be reinjected—must be evaluated against PFAS concentration reduction benefits. Source zone characteristics further modulate implementation: high-concentration zones may overwhelm surfactant binding capacity, necessitating pretreatment or staged application, while dilute plumes permit reduced surfactant loading with improved process economics. Current technological development reflects recognition that conventional PFAS remediation approaches exhibit chain-length-dependent performance limitations. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation establishes foundational understanding toward expanding effective treatment range, though substantial performance gaps remain for short-chain species. Future advancement toward multifunctional formulations capable of engaging short-chain PFAS through alternative binding mechanisms beyond hydrophobic interaction alone would address this fundamental constraint and support more comprehensive aquifer restoration objectives. Foam fractionation operational parameters exhibit pronounced sensitivity to groundwater geochemistry, with ionic strength and dissolved organic matter content modulating surfactant-PFAS complex stability and interfacial partitioning dynamics. Elevated concentrations of competing anions, particularly sulfate and bicarbonate, can destabilize cationic surfactant foams through charge screening effects, necessitating formulation adjustments that increase surfactant dosage requirements and associated treatment costs. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation systematically examined these matrix effects across representative groundwater compositions, establishing operational envelopes within which chain-length-dependent removal efficiencies remain achievable. Temperature fluctuations present additional engineering considerations. Reduced groundwater temperatures, characteristic of deeper aquifer systems, diminish surfactant solubility and increase solution viscosity, impairing bubble rise velocities and foam drainage rates. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures enhance surfactant mobility but accelerate foam collapse, reducing PFAS enrichment factors. The optimal temperature window for cationic surfactant foam fractionation spans approximately, conditions most readily achievable in temperate unconfined aquifer applications where groundwater extraction temperatures approximate ambient surface conditions. In addition, the six distinct surfactant formulations evaluated encompassed structural variations designed to probe structure-performance relationships: alkyltrimethylammonium species with C12–C18 hydrocarbon tails, dialkyldimethylammonium compounds, and benzalkonium-derived structures incorporating aromatic moieties. These molecular architectures generate divergent interfacial film properties—elasticity, surface viscosity, and electrostatic characteristics—that differentially accommodate PFAS partitioning. Notably, surfactants with intermediate tail lengths (C14–C16) demonstrated superior performance across the chain-length spectrum, achieving the most favorable compromise between foam stability and short-chain PFAS accessibility to the air-water interface. Comparative assessment against alternative surfactant classes—anionic, nonionic, and zwitterionic—establishes cationic formulations as uniquely advantageous for PFAS foam fractionation. Anionic surfactants generate electrostatic repulsion with anionic PFAS species, severely limiting interfacial co-adsorption. Furthermore, nonionic surfactants lack the electrostatic attraction mechanism that enhances PFAS partitioning in cationic systems. Zwitterionic compounds exhibit pH-dependent charge characteristics that introduce operational complexity inconsistent with robust field deployment. Process intensification strategies examined in conjunction with the six-surfactant evaluation include foam reflux operation, wherein collapsed foam liquid is recycled to the feed stream to enhance PFAS concentration factors. Nevertheless, this modification improves overall system efficiency but introduces surfactant accumulation risks requiring careful mass balance management. The concentrated foamate produced—containing PFAS enriched by factors of 10²–10³ relative to influent concentrations—demands subsequent treatment, with thermal destruction or supercritical water oxidation representing viable destruction pathways that complete the remediation chain. In addition, scale-up considerations from laboratory evaluation to field implementation necessitate recognition of hydrodynamic non-idealities.
